metadata.title: The Edge Budget That Unlocks Dynamic Programming on Graphs
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = edge budget / A[i,V] / unambiguous ordering; CRIMSON = circular dependency / no natural order / naive approach fails
metadata.note: Exclusions honored: no pseudocode, no negative cycle detection, no APSP extension. Wonder register.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged

swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: med davy A-I. From Sanskrit — meaning intelligent, or intellectually brilliant. The perfect name for our A-I-powered intelligent learning systems.

props.headline: Medhavy AI
props.body:
मेधावी (Medhavy): From Sanskrit, meaning
"intelligent" or "intellectually brilliant"
— the perfect name for our AI-powered
intelligent learning systems.

beats.narration_text: Dynamic programming works beautifully on sequences — there's a left-to-right order. A graph has arrows going everywhere. How do you define a smaller subproblem on a structure with no natural direction?

card.copy: The Edge Budget That Unlocks Dynamic Programming on Graphs

beats.narration_text: Dynamic programming requires subproblems with an unambiguous ordering: each must depend only on strictly smaller subproblems. Graphs appear to violate this — you can follow edges in circles.

production_viz.label: Graph with circular arrows showing dependency cycles; CRIMSON for circular dependency; sequence with clear left-to-right order in TEAL for contrast

beats.narration_text: Graphs have no natural ordering that makes subproblems smaller. What second parameter do you add to the problem definition to create an unambiguous ordering?

card.copy: Shortest path on a graph: circular dependencies everywhere. What parameter creates an unambiguous 'smaller subproblem' for DP?
card.sub: The edge budget is the key

beats.narration_text: Add an edge budget: the maximum number of edges allowed. Define A[i, V] as the shortest path from the source to vertex V using at most i edges. Now 'smaller' means 'fewer edges allowed' — strictly ordered.

production_viz.label: A[i,V] table with i=edge budget and V=vertex; 'smaller i' is strictly unambiguous; TEAL for the ordering parameter i

beats.narration_text: The recurrence has two cases. Case 1: optimal path uses fewer than i edges → A[i,V] equals A[i-1,V], inherit previous. Case 2: uses exactly i edges → crosses one last edge from neighbor W to V: A[i,V] equals A[i-1,W] plus cost of edge W to V.

production_viz.label: Two-case branching diagram: Case 1 inherit A[i-1,V] (TEAL), Case 2 last hop from W: A[i-1,W]+cost (TEAL); min of both taken

beats.narration_text: Take the minimum over both cases and all possible last-hop neighbors W. This is the Bellman-Ford recurrence. The budget i runs from 0 to n minus 1. At most n minus 1 edges suffice for any shortest path without a cycle.

production_viz.label: A[i,V] = min(A[i-1,V], min_W{A[i-1,W]+cost}) displayed; n-1 rounds bound labeled; TEAL for Bellman-Ford recurrence

beats.narration_text: Building the table: A[0, source] equals 0; A[0, all others] equals infinity. Round 1: direct edges from source. Round 2: two-hop paths. Continue for n minus 1 rounds.

production_viz.label: DP table filling round by round: i=0 (source=0, others=∞), i=1 (direct edges), i=2 (two-hop); TEAL cells filling in; CRIMSON for ∞ unfilled cells

beats.narration_text: On a 5-vertex graph, after 4 rounds the table contains the shortest path to every vertex. No global coordination needed — each entry depends only on the previous round.

production_viz.label: 5-vertex graph s,A,B,C,T with DP table fully filled after 4 rounds; TEAL for final shortest path values; each entry from previous round only

beats.narration_text: The edge budget trick generalizes. Whenever a natural ordering seems absent, look for a resource that gets consumed — steps, fuel, cost, time. That resource becomes the ordering parameter.

production_viz.label: Generalizable trick: edge budget → steps, fuel, cost, time as ordering parameters; TEAL for the general principle; examples labeled

beats.narration_text: A[i,V] equals shortest path using at most i edges. Two cases: inherit A[i-1,V], or last hop from W: A[i-1,W] plus cost. n minus 1 rounds suffice. Edge budget creates the unambiguous ordering dynamic programming requires.

card.copy: A[i,V] = shortest path with ≤ i edges. Two cases: inherit previous, or pluck last hop. n-1 rounds suffice. Edge budget = the ordering that makes DP work on graphs.
card.sub: The edge budget unlocks DP on graphs

beats.narration_text: med davy. A-I-powered intelligent learning systems. On YouTube, at med davy A-I. med davy dot com.
